animations.alt_text: One arm rotates around a vertex so the angle changes from right to obtuse to acute.
animations.description: Synthetic preview entry for angle vocabulary, angle comparison, and classroom-ready metadata.
misconceptions: A right angle can face any direction and still remain a right angle.
Pupils may think an obtuse angle is any angle that looks large.

nc_references.statement: Describe, sketch and draw using conventional terms and notation

animations.title: Definitions X–XII — right, obtuse, and acute angles

animations.alt_text: An equilateral-triangle construction creates a vertical line from a point on a base line.
animations.description: Synthetic preview entry for perpendicular constructions and right-angle reasoning.
misconceptions: Perpendicular lines are defined by the right angle they make, not by looking vertical.
Pupils may place the perpendicular by eye rather than by construction.

nc_references.statement: Identify horizontal and vertical lines and pairs of perpendicular and parallel lines
Derive and use the standard ruler and compass constructions

animations.title: Proposition XI — draw a perpendicular from a point on a line

animations.alt_text: The three angles of a triangle are rearranged along a straight line to show a 180-degree total.
animations.description: Synthetic preview entry for triangle angle sums, exterior angles, and missing-angle work.
misconceptions: The triangle's angles still total 180 degrees even when the triangle is scalene or obtuse.
Pupils may think the angle sum changes when the triangle changes shape.

nc_references.statement: Find unknown angles in triangles, quadrilaterals and regular polygons
Derive and use the sum of angles in a triangle and use it to deduce the angle sum in polygons

animations.title: Proposition XXXII — angle sum of a triangle
